pageMeta.title: AI Agents for Fantasy Sports Support and Operations
pageMeta.description: DocsBot helps fantasy sports platforms answer rule, roster, contest, and support questions faster while routing complex player issues to your team.

hero.eyebrow: Source-grounded AI agents for fantasy sports platforms
hero.headline: Run faster fantasy sports support with DocsBot AI agents
hero.subheadline: DocsBot helps fantasy sports management teams create AI agents trained on your rules, scoring guides, FAQs, onboarding docs, policies, and support history. Give players instant answers, help staff find internal procedures, and trigger scoped workflows when live contest or account context is connected.

proofBullets.text: Answer scoring, draft, waiver, league, payout, and contest questions from approved fantasy sports documentation.
Use customer-configured Skills, APIs, connectors, or MCP options to look up live context and route support requests.
Surface repeated player questions, unresolved topics, and documentation gaps through analytics, logs, and conversation review.
Escalate sensitive account, payment, compliance, or responsible gaming issues to human support with conversation context.

hero.primaryCta: Build your fantasy sports AI agent

businessClarity.whatDocsBotDoes: DocsBot turns your fantasy sports knowledge base into an AI support and operations agent. Train it on game rules, scoring systems, lineup policies, contest terms, responsible play content, help articles, SOPs, and internal runbooks so players and staff can get fast, source-grounded answers without digging through long docs.

whoItHelps.roleContext: Support teams field repetitive questions during drafts, lock times, waiver runs, injuries, scoring corrections, deposits, withdrawals, and playoff deadlines. DocsBot can answer routine questions from approved sources and prepare escalations when a player needs a person.

bullets.text: Deflect common rule, lineup, standings, and contest-format questions before they become tickets.
Draft helpdesk replies from connected ticket history and your current support policies when configured.
Escalate account, payment, eligibility, or dispute issues with a concise conversation summary.

whoItHelps.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders
whoItHelps.roleContext: Fantasy sports products change quickly across seasons, sports, contests, promotions, and rule variants. DocsBot helps teams keep player-facing guidance searchable and identify the topics users struggle to understand.

bullets.text: Make scoring guides, roster rules, draft instructions, and promotion terms easier to query from one agent.
Use analytics and conversation review to spot repeated questions after releases, rule updates, or slate changes.
Support multilingual player education for global sports communities with answers in more than 100 languages.

whoItHelps.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams
whoItHelps.roleContext: Real-money and daily fantasy sports teams need clear boundaries around account status, eligibility, taxation, responsible gaming, and dispute handling. DocsBot can provide approved policy guidance while routing high-impact decisions to humans.

bullets.text: Point players to responsible gaming, self-exclusion, eligibility, and account-safety content from approved sources.
Keep sensitive actions bounded with permissions, logs, customer-defined controls, and human review paths where needed.
Maintain consistent internal guidance for support teams handling regulated or state-specific questions.

evidenceSources.metric: 62.5 million fantasy sports players in the U.S. and Canada in 2022
evidenceSources.claim: The Fantasy Sports & Gaming Association reported 62.5 million fantasy sports players in the U.S. and Canada in 2022, underscoring the scale of support and education needs for fantasy platforms.
evidenceSources.sourceTitle: Fantasy Sports & Gaming Industry - Fantasy Sports & Gaming Association

evidenceSources.metric: Sports betting legal in 38 states and Washington, D.C.
evidenceSources.claim: The American Gaming Association says sports betting is legal in 38 states and Washington, D.C., illustrating the fragmented regulatory environment surrounding adjacent fantasy and sports gaming products.
evidenceSources.sourceTitle: Supporting the Legal Sports Betting Market - American Gaming Association

evidenceSources.metric: IRS Chief Counsel memorandum addresses daily fantasy sports and wagering excise taxes
evidenceSources.claim: IRS Office of Chief Counsel memorandum AM 2020-009 states that a daily fantasy sports operator can be liable for federal wagering excise tax under IRC section 4401.
evidenceSources.sourceTitle: Office of Chief Counsel Memorandum AM 2020-009 - Daily Fantasy Sports and the Excise Taxes on Wagering

evidenceSources.metric: NCPG standards cover policy, staff training, player help, self-exclusion, advertising, site features, research, and payments
evidenceSources.claim: The National Council on Problem Gambling’s Internet Responsible Gambling Standards include recommendations for operators, regulators, and vendors across policy, staff training, informed decision-making, player assistance, self-exclusion, advertising, site features, and payments.
evidenceSources.sourceTitle: Internet Responsible Gambling Standards - National Council on Problem Gambling

evidenceSources.metric: Colorado fantasy contest rules require responsible gaming links and self-exclusion policy references
evidenceSources.claim: Colorado’s fantasy contest provider rule requires website or mobile app responsible gaming information, including links to help resources and the provider’s self-exclusion program.
evidenceSources.sourceTitle: 1 CCR 207-3-5 - Responsible Gaming and Self Exclusion

agentUseCases.name: Player rules and scoring support agent
agentUseCases.problem: Players often ask the same urgent questions about scoring, lineup locks, waivers, ties, roster eligibility, late scratches, and playoff rules, especially around drafts and live slates.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: Train DocsBot on league rules, scoring charts, FAQs, contest terms, help articles, and release notes. The agent answers with source-grounded explanations, asks clarifying questions when a sport or contest format matters, and escalates disputes or edge cases to support.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: With approved Skills, APIs, connectors, or customer-configured workflows, the agent can look up a player’s contest type, league settings, or ticket status before answering or routing the request.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: Why did my quarterback score change after the game ended?
Can I move a player after the lineup lock?
How are ties handled in this best-ball contest?
Where do I find waiver priority rules for my league?
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: Players get faster rule clarity, support teams spend less time repeating policy explanations, and operations leaders can see which rules need clearer documentation.

agentUseCases.name: Draft, onboarding, and contest education agent
agentUseCases.problem: New players need help understanding contest formats, salary caps, drafts, roster construction, and payout structures before they feel confident joining paid or free contests.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: DocsBot can guide users through your approved onboarding content, draft guides, glossary, contest pages, eligibility terms, and responsible play resources. It can collect intent, recommend the right help article, and hand off account-specific questions.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: Configured workflows can collect lead or onboarding details, create a support ticket, notify community teams, or route a user to a demo, tutorial, or human support queue.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: What is the difference between snake draft and salary cap contests?
Which beginner contests should I read about first?
How do playoffs work in a private league?
Can you summarize this contest’s payout rules?
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: New users learn the product faster, content teams reuse existing guides more effectively, and support teams see fewer basic onboarding tickets.

agentUseCases.name: Responsible play and eligibility guidance agent
agentUseCases.problem: Fantasy sports and adjacent sports gaming teams need consistent answers about age, location, eligibility, self-exclusion, responsible gaming tools, and account limits without letting AI make sensitive determinations.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: Train the agent on approved responsible gaming pages, eligibility policies, state-specific notices, self-exclusion instructions, support escalation procedures, and internal compliance guidance. The agent provides policy-grounded information and routes high-impact cases to trained staff.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: When authorized, a Skill can check broad account-status metadata, create a responsible-play ticket, or send a handoff summary while keeping eligibility decisions and account restrictions under human or system-of-record control.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: Where can I find your self-exclusion tools?
Why is my state not eligible for this contest?
How do I set limits or take a break?
Who can review my account restriction?
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: Players receive consistent responsible-play guidance, while compliance-sensitive requests stay within scoped workflows and human review boundaries.

agentUseCases.name: Contest operations and incident response assistant
agentUseCases.problem: When scoring feeds, stat corrections, settlement timing, or promotional rules generate confusion, operations teams need a quick way to brief support, community, and product teams from the same source of truth.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: DocsBot can act as a private internal knowledge agent trained on incident runbooks, release notes, stat-provider policies, escalation paths, and customer communication templates. Staff can ask what changed, what to say, and who owns the next step.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: Customer-configured Skills can summarize an incident, create a task, update approved fields, notify Slack or Microsoft Teams, and route unresolved cases to the right operations owner.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: What is the approved response for tonight’s stat correction delay?
Which teams need to review a payout dispute?
Summarize the known issue for support agents.
Create a follow-up task for the contest operations lead.
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: Teams respond with consistent language during time-sensitive contests and reduce the confusion caused by scattered runbooks and message threads.

agentUseCases.name: Support analytics and knowledge-gap discovery
agentUseCases.problem: Fantasy sports platforms generate seasonal spikes in questions, but many teams struggle to identify which rules, promotions, and product flows are causing the most confusion.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: DocsBot provides logs, analytics, reports, satisfaction and resolution analysis, topic management, and knowledge-gap discovery so teams can review what players ask, what the agent answered, and where documentation needs improvement.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: Teams can export insights into content planning, support operations, or ticket review workflows using customer-configured connectors and approved reporting processes.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: Which waiver questions increased after the last release?
What player questions are missing from our help center?
Show unresolved topics about payouts this week.
Which contest formats create the most support confusion?
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: Content and support leaders can improve documentation before peak sports moments and reduce repeat questions over time.

agentUseCases.name: Daily fantasy tax and policy knowledge assistant
agentUseCases.problem: Daily fantasy sports operators may need finance, legal, and support teams to understand internal guidance around federal wagering tax, contest classification, and approved customer-facing language.
agentUseCases.agentWorkflow: DocsBot can train on internal tax memos, legal guidance, finance SOPs, and approved scripts so employees can retrieve the right source-grounded guidance. The agent should not provide legal or tax advice to players; it can route those questions to the appropriate team.
agentUseCases.liveContextOrTools: Configured actions can create a compliance review task, summarize a support thread, or route a policy question to finance, legal, or operations with the relevant sources attached.
exampleQuestionsOrRequests: Where is our internal guidance on DFS excise tax questions?
What approved language should support use for tax-form questions?
Who reviews contest classification updates?
Summarize this policy issue for legal review.
agentUseCases.businessOutcome: Internal teams spend less time searching for sensitive guidance while keeping final legal, tax, and compliance decisions with qualified humans.

customerFacingAgent.headline: Give fantasy players instant answers without losing control of the rules
customerFacingAgent.intro: A DocsBot customer-facing agent can sit in your website or product chat widget and answer from your approved fantasy sports knowledge. It helps players understand contests, rules, scoring, onboarding, and support paths while escalating account-specific or sensitive cases.

requests.request: “Why did my score change after the game?”
requests.agentResponseStrategy: Explain stat corrections, settlement timing, and scoring rules from your help center, then ask for contest context or route a dispute if the player needs account-specific review.
requests.optionalAction: Create or route a support ticket with the player’s summary if the connected workflow is approved.

requests.request: “Am I eligible to enter this contest from my state?”
requests.agentResponseStrategy: Provide approved eligibility guidance and state-specific notices from your policy library without making sensitive account determinations in the chat.
requests.optionalAction: Escalate to compliance or support with the user’s question and relevant policy references.

requests.request: “How do I set a limit or self-exclude?”
requests.agentResponseStrategy: Point the player to your responsible gaming tools, self-exclusion instructions, and help resources using approved language and visible support paths.
requests.optionalAction: Start a responsible-play handoff or ticket according to your configured workflow boundaries.

requests.request: “Which contest format should I learn first?”
requests.agentResponseStrategy: Summarize beginner-friendly contest formats from your onboarding guides and link users to the right tutorial or rule page for deeper reading.
requests.optionalAction: Collect tutorial interest or route the user to a configured onboarding flow.

requests.agentResponseStrategy: Answer from approved promotion terms, eligibility notes, expiration dates, and payout rules, then escalate ambiguous cases to support.
requests.optionalAction: Create a ticket or handoff summary when the promotion question involves account status or disputed credit.

requests.agentResponseStrategy: Use DocsBot’s multilingual support to answer from the same approved sources while preserving the platform’s guidance and escalation rules.
requests.optionalAction: Route to a multilingual human support queue when configured or when the issue is sensitive.

internalAgent.headline: Keep fantasy operations, support, and compliance teams aligned
internalAgent.intro: DocsBot can also run as a private internal knowledge agent for fantasy sports employees. Train it on runbooks, policies, release notes, incident procedures, ticket history exports, product docs, and legal-approved guidance so teams can move faster with consistent answers.

workflows.name: Support playbook retrieval
workflows.problem: Agents need fast, consistent answers during game windows, but policies may live across docs, ticket history, chats, and spreadsheets.
workflows.agentWorkflow: DocsBot answers staff questions from support SOPs, product docs, FAQs, and approved ticket sources. It can draft replies, cite source content, and identify missing documentation.
workflows.optionalAction: Draft a helpdesk response or route a complex issue through a customer-configured helpdesk workflow.
workflows.outcome: Support teams respond faster with consistent guidance and fewer internal pings.

workflows.name: Incident and stat-correction briefing
workflows.problem: Scoring corrections and feed issues can trigger many player contacts at once, requiring support, product, and operations to speak from the same script.
workflows.agentWorkflow: The internal agent summarizes known issues, retrieves incident runbooks, explains approved player messaging, and shows escalation ownership.
workflows.optionalAction: Notify a team channel, create a task, or generate a handoff summary using approved Skills or workflow automation.
workflows.outcome: Teams coordinate player communication faster during time-sensitive sports moments.

workflows.name: Compliance and responsible gaming knowledge base
workflows.problem: State-by-state rules, responsible gaming policies, and internal review paths are difficult for frontline teams to memorize.
workflows.agentWorkflow: DocsBot retrieves approved guidance for eligibility, self-exclusion, responsible play, account restrictions, and escalation steps while keeping sensitive determinations out of unsupported chat responses.
workflows.optionalAction: Create a compliance review task or summarize a case for a trained reviewer when configured.
workflows.outcome: Frontline teams can find current policy guidance while sensitive decisions remain under defined controls.

workflows.name: Content and product feedback loop
workflows.problem: Product and content teams need to know where players get confused after new contests, releases, or seasonal rule updates.
workflows.agentWorkflow: DocsBot analytics, logs, topic management, and knowledge-gap discovery show repeated questions, weak answer areas, and missing documentation topics.
workflows.optionalAction: Create content backlog tasks or notify documentation owners through customer-configured workflows.
workflows.outcome: Teams can improve help content based on real player questions rather than guesswork.

docsbotFeatureMapping.industryApplication: Place a player-facing agent near rules, contests, and help pages, while giving support and operations teams a private assistant for SOPs, incidents, and policy lookups.

docsbotFeatureMapping.feature: AI Actions, Skills, APIs, connectors, and MCP connectivity

docsbotFeatureMapping.mainLandingPageContext: DocsBot supports SOC 2 Type II certification, GDPR-ready governance, encryption in transit and at rest, granular access controls, role-based permissions, and customer-defined controls for Skills.
docsbotFeatureMapping.industryApplication: Set boundaries for account, payment, eligibility, responsible gaming, legal, or tax workflows while keeping credentials out of chats and preserving human review for sensitive decisions.

trustClaims: Built for high-volume support moments when player questions spike around games, drafts, and releases.
Grounds answers in your owned content library instead of generic fantasy sports advice.
Can assist both website users and human support teams through connected, customer-configured workflows.
trustAndProof.whyTheseReviewsFit: Fantasy sports platforms need fast player support, accurate rules guidance, and a practical path for human teams to handle exceptions. DocsBot proof spans high-volume inquiry handling, owned-content training, embedded website assistance, and support-team workflows that match the pace of sports-driven demand.

schemaHints.serviceType: AI support and internal knowledge agents for fantasy sports management platforms

faq.question: Can DocsBot handle our peak support spikes during drafts, lineup locks, and scoring corrections?
faq.answer: Yes. DocsBot can answer repetitive questions from your approved rules, scoring guides, roster policies, FAQs, and support content before they become tickets. For disputes, payment issues, account-specific scoring reviews, or sensitive cases, it can collect context and route the player to a human support workflow instead of trying to resolve everything itself.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: How do we keep the agent from giving generic fantasy advice instead of our actual contest rules?
faq.answer: DocsBot is designed to answer from your owned knowledge sources, not broad fantasy sports opinions. You can train it on your scoring charts, league settings, contest terms, promotion rules, eligibility language, help center articles, SOPs, and release notes so answers stay grounded in approved materials and reflect your platform’s specific rules.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: Can the agent explain why a player’s score changed after a game without creating more disputes?
faq.answer: DocsBot can explain your published stat-correction policy, settlement timing, scoring rules, and dispute process in plain language. If the question requires account, contest, or transaction review, the agent can ask for the missing context and escalate with a concise summary so support agents do not start from scratch.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: What happens when a player asks about eligibility, self-exclusion, limits, or responsible play?
faq.answer: DocsBot can provide approved responsible-play and eligibility guidance while keeping high-impact determinations with trained staff or systems of record. This matters because NCPG’s Internet Responsible Gambling Standards cover areas such as policy, staff training, player assistance, self-exclusion, site features, advertising, research, and payments.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: Can we use DocsBot for state-specific fantasy contest guidance without letting AI make legal decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. You can train DocsBot on state notices, eligibility policies, contest terms, and escalation SOPs so it retrieves approved guidance and routes uncertain or sensitive cases to compliance. That boundary is important because sports gaming and adjacent fantasy operations often face fragmented state-by-state requirements; sports betting is legal in 38 states and Washington, D.C.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: Will DocsBot integrate with our contest platform, helpdesk, or league settings database?
faq.answer: DocsBot can work with customer-configured Skills, APIs, connectors, MCP options, metadata bindings, or workflow automations where you authorize access. Typical uses include looking up contest type, league settings, ticket status, or broad account metadata; creating a ticket; routing a request; notifying a team; or preparing a handoff. It should be scoped to approved actions, not open-ended system access.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: Can DocsBot help our support agents draft replies in our approved tone?
faq.answer: Yes. When trained on support SOPs, help articles, macros, policy language, and relevant ticket history exports, DocsBot can draft source-grounded replies for human review. This is especially useful for repetitive topics like lineup locks, waivers, promotions, payout timing, stat corrections, private league settings, and contest-format explanations.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: How much content do we need before launching a useful fantasy sports support agent?
faq.answer: You can start with your highest-volume support content: scoring rules, roster and lineup policies, contest terms, payout FAQs, promotion terms, responsible play pages, eligibility notices, and escalation SOPs. Many teams begin with a focused player-facing agent for common questions, then expand into internal runbooks, incident workflows, and analytics-driven knowledge-gap improvements.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: How do we prevent outdated contest rules or expired promotions from being answered?
faq.answer: The main control is content governance. Keep promotion terms, rule pages, release notes, and help articles current, and remove or archive outdated sources from the agent’s training set. DocsBot logs, conversation review, topic management, and knowledge-gap discovery can help your team spot questions where users are seeing confusion or where documentation needs cleanup.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: Can DocsBot support both free season-long fantasy and real-money daily fantasy operations?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as each experience is grounded in the right source set and workflow boundaries. A free season-long agent might focus on drafts, waivers, trades, playoffs, and league settings, while a DFS-focused agent may need stricter escalation for eligibility, payouts, tax language, responsible play, and contest disputes.
faq.role: General buyer

faq.question: Can the same agent support players and internal operations teams?
faq.answer: It is usually better to separate experiences. A player-facing agent can answer from approved public help content and route sensitive cases, while a private internal agent can access SOPs, incident runbooks, release notes, compliance guidance, and support playbooks. That separation helps prevent employee-only guidance from appearing in player conversations.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: What should stay with a human instead of being automated?
faq.answer: Keep humans in control of account restrictions, payment disputes, eligibility decisions, tax or legal interpretation, responsible-play interventions, fraud reviews, contest settlement exceptions, and high-value player escalations. DocsBot is best used to explain approved policy, gather context, summarize the issue, cite relevant sources, and route the case to the right queue or reviewer.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: Can DocsBot answer multilingual player questions during global sports events?
faq.answer: Yes. DocsBot can answer in more than 100 languages while grounding responses in the same approved rules, help articles, and policies. For sensitive issues such as eligibility, payments, account access, or responsible play, you can configure the agent to route the player to a multilingual human support path when available.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: How does DocsBot help us measure whether the agent is actually reducing support load?
faq.answer: DocsBot provides analytics, reports, logs, conversation review, satisfaction and resolution analysis, topic management, and knowledge-gap discovery. Teams can review which questions were answered, which topics still escalated, where players were dissatisfied, and which rules or product flows need better documentation before the next sports peak.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: What KPIs should we track for a fantasy sports AI support rollout?
faq.answer: Track ticket deflection for repetitive rule questions, first-response time, escalation rate, resolution rate, player satisfaction, unresolved topics, peak-window containment, repeat-contact rate, documentation gaps, and support agent handle time. For regulated or sensitive workflows, also review escalation accuracy, policy adherence, and whether the agent avoided unsupported account or eligibility decisions.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: Can DocsBot help us identify which rules are confusing players the most?
faq.answer: Yes. Conversation logs, topic management, analytics, and knowledge-gap discovery can show repeated questions around waivers, scoring, lineup locks, payouts, promotions, state eligibility, or contest formats. Product and content teams can use those insights to update help articles, improve onboarding, clarify release notes, or adjust in-product guidance.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: How would DocsBot support an incident like delayed stat corrections or a settlement issue?
faq.answer: For internal teams, DocsBot can retrieve incident runbooks, approved player messaging, escalation ownership, status notes, and support playbooks. With configured Skills or workflows, it can help summarize the incident, create a task, notify a team channel, or draft a support response for human review.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: Can DocsBot help support teams during NFL Sunday or other high-volume game windows?
faq.answer: Yes. Fantasy platforms serve a large audience; the Fantasy Sports & Gaming Association reported 62.5 million fantasy sports players in the U.S. and Canada in 2022. DocsBot can reduce repetitive support pressure by answering approved rules, scoring, roster, and contest questions instantly while routing disputes and account-specific issues to staff.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: How do we handle tax-form or DFS excise-tax questions with an AI agent?
faq.answer: Use DocsBot as an internal knowledge and routing assistant, not a source of legal or tax advice to players. It can retrieve approved finance, legal, and support guidance, draft policy-consistent language for review, and route complex questions. The IRS Chief Counsel memorandum AM 2020-009 states that a DFS operator can be liable for federal wagering excise tax under IRC section 4401.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: What security controls matter if the agent can look up live account or contest context?
faq.answer: Use scoped Skills, customer-defined permissions, logging, role-based access, and clear human-review paths. DocsBot supports SOC 2 Type II certification, GDPR-ready governance, encryption in transit and at rest, granular access controls, role-based permissions, and customer-managed controls for Skills so sensitive workflows can be bounded.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: Can procurement compare DocsBot to building our own RAG support tool?
faq.answer: Yes. The main comparison points are time to launch, content-source management, analytics, logs, human escalation, governance controls, multilingual support, website or product chat deployment, and workflow actions. Building in-house may offer custom control, but teams also need to maintain retrieval quality, permissions, evaluation, monitoring, and support operations over time.
faq.role: General buyer

faq.question: How long does implementation usually take for a first fantasy sports use case?
faq.answer: A focused rollout can start with a contained knowledge set such as scoring rules, contest FAQs, payout policies, and escalation SOPs. The practical timeline depends on content readiness, approval cycles, security review, brand tone, testing, and whether you want live lookups or helpdesk workflows in the first phase.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders

faq.question: What will our support agents need to learn before using DocsBot internally?
faq.answer: Agents should learn when to trust source-grounded answers, how to review citations or referenced content, when to escalate, how to use drafted replies, and which topics are restricted. Training should emphasize that DocsBot assists with retrieval, summaries, and response drafting, while humans still own judgment for disputes, payments, eligibility, responsible play, and account actions.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: Can DocsBot route different questions to support, compliance, game operations, or payments?
faq.answer: Yes, if you configure the routing rules and workflows. DocsBot can classify the request, collect missing context, summarize the conversation, and route or create a task through approved Skills, APIs, connectors, or workflow automations. Common routing paths include scoring disputes, payment questions, account access, responsible play, promotion issues, and incident response.
faq.role: Player support and community teams

faq.question: How do we make sure compliance can audit what the agent said?
faq.answer: DocsBot provides logs and conversation review so teams can inspect player interactions, escalations, unresolved topics, and answer quality. For sensitive fantasy sports workflows, buyers should define review processes for eligibility, self-exclusion, payments, tax language, and disputed outcomes so compliance can verify that the agent followed approved content and escalation rules.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: What content should not be exposed to a player-facing agent?
faq.answer: Do not expose internal fraud signals, private incident notes, employee-only escalation rules, legal deliberations, tax memos, security procedures, vendor credentials, or unpublished product plans to a public agent. Use separate knowledge sources and permissions for player-facing support versus internal operations so the right audience sees the right guidance.
faq.role: Compliance, trust, and responsible play teams

faq.question: How can DocsBot help content teams justify documentation work after launch?
faq.answer: DocsBot can show recurring questions, unresolved topics, weak answer areas, and gaps in your help center or rules documentation. Instead of guessing which articles to rewrite, content and product teams can prioritize based on actual player confusion around drafts, waivers, lineup locks, promotions, scoring corrections, eligibility, and payouts.
faq.role: Product, content, and game operations leaders
